Anatomy of a Buyer (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 1 of 4
Speaker 1: Hey guys, welcome back to the final lesson of Module Two. So we've done quite a bit of
groundwork up to now. We've learnt the Coaching the Conversion Process, we've gotten
an overview of the journey between point A and point Buyer, and we even got super
intimate and close with our customer, and hopefully dug up some really amazing
insights in those exercises that we just did together.
And in Module Three, we're going to really start putting it all together and executing the
copy, and the messaging that activates these things we just spoke about. But as a final,
final step before we do that, I want us to ask a really simple question. I want us to ask,
what is a buyer?
Yes, it's true that a buyer is someone who pulls their credit card out and hits an order
form and clicks buy. But what is really happening internally? What would happen if we
put a buyer on an operating table and started dissecting it to see the actually
ingredients and makeup? And that's really the goal of this quick lesson, it's to see some
of those invisible triggers at play. What's shifted? What is the substratum of buying
activity? What are these parts that make a whole? In short, we're gonna be starting with
the end in mind and then reverse engineering those pieces to make sure that, by the
end of our funnel, we've literally constructed a buyer. Cool.
So what are these ingredients that makeup a buyer? A buyer is someone who has an
active interest in solving the problem or achieving the outcome your product delivers.
And that's kind of basic, yet it's our job to get them more intimately acquainted with
that desire, as well as their newfound ability to solve it. But if it's not already there at
least as a seed, it's not dormant, then we won't have much luck.
However, beyond the outcome our product solves, our buyer is also someone who's
been given the opportunity to leverage and express their unique gifts and
circumstances, aka cash in on the exclusivity points. Now this comes back to asking the
question we did earlier. In what ways is my prospect exclusively empowered to use and
benefit from my product, or to even embark on this journey? And it's a super powerful
buyer's trait that we want to take full advantage of.
So when we do this, and we do this well, our audience feels, our prospect feels like the
product was created just for them and their unique makeup and situation. They get to
leverage these things they're proud of. Leverage their current situation. Even leverage
past failures, in order to get the result. And that is a super powerful trigger that makes
up a buyer.
So you'll want to be super specific in your qualifiers, and you'll want to raise the bar for
your best customers to have the satisfaction of vaulting over it. So, you'll see a little
later the perfect for you template in the closing subsequence, or actually the sales
sequence will be one of the main tools that you use to leverage this trigger.
But moving on, a buyer is also someone who is intimately aware of the cost of not taking
action. You know, we talk a lot about risk reversal. You're gonna learn something a little
later called risk revealer, which talks all about getting them acquainted with exactly
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 4 --

Anatomy of a Buyer (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 2 of 4
what happens if they don't even make a decision right now, they allow themselves to
continue going in as status quo. You know, you don't need to fully mitigate the risk if
you can show that the bigger risk actually comes with inaction.
A buyer also has renewed belief and optimism that the outcome can be achieved, and
more specifically that they can do it. And this all starts with what we spoke about
earlier, which is grounding those aspirations in the complete understanding and
validation of their current reality. Unless you ground those lofty outcomes, it becomes
very hard to believe that this is achievable, and that this is achievable for them. So that's
where confidence, and trust, and just validating, and reflecting back to their current
reality really, really goes a long way.
But a buyer is also someone who can enjoy the payoff of vindicating past mistakes, or
reframing them with new meaning. There was a movie that came out a few years ago, it
wasn't a very popular one, but it was called Someday This Pain Will Be Useful. And that's
a very healing and powerful human drive, is to just be able to reframe past failures, past
things that we weren't so proud of. And that becomes included in the vindication. So for
someone who can enjoy the payoff of vindicating past failures, is a very powerful
trigger, and a very powerful part of the makeup of a successful buyer.
But a buyer is also someone who feels in control and empowered. Now, we resist
actions that we feel are being forced upon us. We resist and get into self protection
mode whenever we feel like we're being backed into a corner and not given a choice. So
a buyer, on the contrary, is someone who feels like they're asserting their own will on
the situation. That they're acting within their own volition. That they're not being
manipulated, or forced, or pushed into a certain decision, but that they're actually
getting to co-create it and make it themselves. So in Module Seven, you're gonna see
something I call The Build Your Offer Close, which really activates some of these triggers
of co-creation of participation. Of feeling like they have enacted their own power in
making that decision.
So a big part in empowering people to assert their will and commit, also involves
lowering the bar. I know we just spoke about raising the bar, we raise the bar as far as
the things ... aspects of their past, qualifications they have, in order to clear it and be
perfect for out product. But we also lower the bar of the commitment, because the
commitment is the scariest part, right?
So, Minimum Viable Commitment, it's a concept we're gonna go deeper into later. But
this is a big, big part of building a buyer. If we want people to become customers, we
need to lower the bar of what that means. Now, if we can redefine a buyer as someone
who doesn't necessarily have unshakeable, ironclad confidence, but just enough to tip
the scales and give it a try, we give our prospects not just a much more attainable, but a
more realistic image of a buyer to relate with.
It's the difference between saying, let's go on a date next week and see where it goes,
versus let's spend the next 70 years, grow old and die together. One of them is this
enormous commitment that requires this ironclad confidence that just isn't realistic,
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 4 --

Anatomy of a Buyer (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 3 of 4
isn't natural to anybody. So we lower the commitment bar. We redefine a buyer as not
someone who has this crazy, unshakeable, focused, ironclad confidence that it's gonna
work and that they're gonna be able to do it, but just enough, just enough to give it a
try. To show up, to play full, and see where that takes them. So lowering the bar, as far
as a Minimum Viable Commitment.
And we're gonna go a lot deeper into this topic in Module Seven and how to apply it.
But for now, before we get there, I just want you to briefly consider what lowering the
bar, as a buyer, might mean for you. How can you make it more passable? How can you
make your prospect feel like their skepticism, their skepticism is welcome, and included,
and actually part of what being a buyer is?
And then finally, the final ingredient is something we've all heard before, but it's worth
repeating. A buyer is someone who is giving you hard earned money, and to do that
they must trust you. They must trust you to get them results. They must trust you to
deliver what you promised. And they must trust you to continue caring about them after
a payment has cleared. And this is quite a vast topic, and this goes beyond testimonials
and case studies. That's one level of trust.
But another level of trust that we're really honing in on in this course, is a powerful way
to build trust being to mirror back their reality accurately, specifically. That's how we
leverage specificity to the highest degree. Specificity, when used in leverage, is an
ingredient for trust. It's a trust that we get them, we see them, we know exactly what
they're experiencing. And therefore, there is that trust in our diagnosis and in our
prescription for that problem. So there's gonna be a whole other lesson on this later, we
won't go too deep into it right now. But, I just want to expand that definition of what
trust is.
And on that note, we are done Module Two. And before we get to Module Three, I'm
just gonna remind you that from here on out we're gonna be going step-by-step, and
really writing out your funnel or your launch campaign. And we're gonna start with
segmentation and confirmation sequences in Module Three.
Now, a prerequisite to move forward is that you've nailed down which launch
methodology you plan to go with. So, we're gonna reference some excellent ones in the
resources section. These are people that we've vetted, that we've communicated with,
and just really have solid, proven launch methodologies. So you could choose one from
the resource section if you haven't done so already, or you could actually dive into our
bonus.
If you're really just starting out and you want to do this as quickly as possible, with as
little layers, and pieces, and moving parts as possibly, I fully encourage you to take a
look at the Minimum Viable Launch Funnel in our bonus section, which is gonna show
you how to do this with email only, using nothing but your email software.
And then of course, you always have the option to, if you are experienced in the field of
building your funnels and your launch strategies, you have every right to just go with
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com
